azure-database-microsoft-oracle-07000.description:
Oracle database found. To migrate a Java application that uses an Oracle database to Azure, you can follow these recommendations:

 * **Migrate to Azure Database for PostgreSQL**: Azure recommends migrating Oracle databases to Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server as it provides better cost-effectiveness and performance. Create a managed PostgreSQL Flexible Server database in Azure and choose the appropriate pricing tier based on your application's requirements.

 * **Use migration tools**: Utilize the Azure Database Migration Service (DMS) or third-party tools to migrate your Oracle database schema and data to PostgreSQL. Consider using ora2pg or similar tools to convert Oracle-specific SQL to PostgreSQL-compatible SQL.

 * **Update database drivers and connection strings**: Replace Oracle JDBC drivers with PostgreSQL drivers in your Java application. Update connection strings from Oracle format (jdbc:oracle:thin:) to PostgreSQL format (jdbc:postgresql:).

 * **Review and convert Oracle-specific code**: Identify and convert Oracle-specific SQL functions, stored procedures, and PL/SQL code to PostgreSQL equivalents. Pay attention to data types, syntax differences, and built-in functions.

 * Enable **monitoring and diagnostics**: Utilize Azure Monitor to gain insights into the performance and health of your Java application and the underlying PostgreSQL database. Set up metrics, alerts, and log analytics to proactively identify and resolve issues.

 * Implement **security** measures: Apply security best practices to protect your Java application and the PostgreSQL database. This includes implementing authentication and authorization mechanisms with passwordless connections and leveraging Microsoft Defender for Cloud for threat detection and vulnerability assessments.

 * **Backup** your data: Azure Database for PostgreSQL provides automated backups by default. You can configure the retention period for backups based on your requirements. You can also enable geo-redundant backups, if needed, to enhance data durability and availability.
azure-database-microsoft-oracle-07000.title: Oracle database found

azure-java-version-01000.description:
The application is using a Java version that has reached the end of support. It is strongly recommended to plan and execute a migration strategy to upgrade your application to a supported Java version.
Supported Java versions receive long-term support (LTS) from the Java community, including bug fixes and updates. Migrating to a supported version provides you with a stable and well-maintained platform for your application.
azure-java-version-01000.title: Java Version Has Reached the End of Support

azure-java-version-02000.description:
The application is not using the latest LTS Java version. It is recommended to consider upgrading to the latest LTS version to take advantage of the newest language features, performance improvements, and extended support timelines.
Upgrading to the latest LTS version ensures your application benefits from the most recent security enhancements and a longer support lifecycle.
azure-java-version-02000.title: Java Version is not the latest LTS

azure-password-01000.description:
Using clear passwords in property files is a security risk, as they can be easily compromised if the files are accessed by unauthorized individuals. To enhance the security of your application, it is recommended to employ secure credential management practices.

 * **Azure Key Vault**: Utilize Azure Key Vault to securely store and manage your application's passwords and other sensitive credentials. Azure Key Vault provides a centralized and highly secure location for storing secrets, keys, and certificates.

 * **Passwordless connections**: You can provide an additional layer of security and convenience for accessing resources in Azure by eliminating the need for passwords. This way you can reduce the risk of password-related vulnerabilities, such as weak passwords or password theft.
azure-password-01000.title: Password found in configuration file

links.title: Read a secret from Azure Key Vault in a Spring Boot application
Azure Spring Boot Starter for Azure Key Vault Secrets

jakarta-database-00002.description:
The application depends on **Jakarta Persistence (JPA)** APIs (`jakarta.persistence.*` or legacy `javax.persistence.*`), which are used for object-relational mapping (ORM) and database interaction in Jakarta EE or Java EE applications.

When migrating to Azure:
- Ensure that the database connection, JPA provider (e.g., Hibernate, EclipseLink), and dialect are compatible with your target Azure database service.
- Recommended database services include **Azure Database for PostgreSQL**, **Azure Database for MySQL**, or **Azure SQL Database**.
- For containerized deployments, these JPA-based applications can run on **Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS)** or **Azure App Service for Linux/Windows**.
- If using **Spring Data JPA**, verify that connection pool settings and environment variables are properly configured for the cloud environment.
- Consider leveraging **Azure Key Vault** for secure storage of database credentials and connection strings.
jakarta-database-00002.title: Detects usage of Jakarta Persistence (JPA) APIs

links.title: Prepare to choose a data store in Azure

spring-boot-to-azure-port-01000.description:
The application is setting the server port. To migrate a Java application that sets the server port to Azure Container Apps:

 * **Azure Container Apps allows you to expose port according to your Azure Container Apps resource configuration. For instance, a Spring Boot application listens to port of 8080 by default, but it can be set with server.port or environment variable SERVER_PORT as you need.
spring-boot-to-azure-port-01000.title: Server port configuration found

links.title: Identify any clients relying on a non-standard port

spring-boot-to-azure-restricted-config-0.description:
The application uses restricted configurations for Azure Container Apps.
 These properties can be automatically injected into your application environment by Azure Container Apps to access managed Config Server and managed Eureka Server.
 Please remove them from your application, including configuration files, config server files, command line parameters, Java system attributes, and environment variables.

 If configured in **configuration files**: they will be ignored and overrided by Azure Container Apps.
 
 If configured in **Config Server files**, **command line parameters**, **Java system attribute**, **environment variable**: they need to be removed or you might experience conflicts and unexpected behavior.
spring-boot-to-azure-restricted-config-0.title: Restricted configurations found

links.title: Migrate Spring Boot applications to Azure Container Apps - Remove restricted configurations
Connect to a managed Config Server for Spring in Azure Container Apps
Connect to a managed Eureka Server for Spring in Azure Container Apps

spring-boot-to-azure-spring-boot-version.description:
The application is using a Spring Boot version that has reached its End of OSS Support.
With the officially supported new versions from Spring, you can get the best experience. Here are some steps you can take to update your application to the latest version of Spring Boot:

* Choose a **supported Spring Boot version**: Check out Spring Boot Support Versions and determine the most suitable supported Spring Boot version.

* **Update Spring Boot version**: Update the Spring Boot version of your application. There are automated tools like Rewrite to help you with the migration.

* **Address code compatibility**: Review your application's codebase for any potential compatibility issues with the target Spring Boot version. Update deprecated APIs or features, address any language or library changes, and ensure that your code follows best practices and standards.

* **Test thoroughly**: Execute a comprehensive testing process to verify the compatibility and functionality of your application with the new Spring Boot version. Perform unit tests, integration tests, and system tests to validate that all components and dependencies work as expected.
spring-boot-to-azure-spring-boot-version.title: Spring Boot Version Has Reached the End of OSS Support

links.title: Migrate Spring Boot applications to Azure Container Apps
Launch your first Java microservice application with managed Java components in Azure Container Apps

spring-framework-version-01000.description:
Your application is using a Spring Framework version that has reached its End of OSS Support.
Upgrading to a supported version ensures better performance, security, and compatibility with modern tools.
  1. Pick a Supported Version: Review the Spring Framework support policy and choose an actively supported version.
  2. Update Your Project: Change the Spring Framework version in your pom.xml or build.gradle.
  3. Fix Compatibility Issues: Update deprecated code, replace removed features, and ensure dependencies are compatible with the new Spring Framework version.
  4. Thoroughly Test: Run unit, integration, and end-to-end tests to make sure everything still works after the upgrade.
spring-framework-version-01000.title: Spring Framework Version Has Reached the End of OSS Support

Database.0003.description:
System.Data.SqlClient dependency is detected.

While System.Data.SqlClient will work in Azure, the newer Microsoft.Data.SqlClient package is the primary .NET SQL client moving forward and receiving the latest features. For example, Microsoft.Data.SqlClient easily allows use of Azure Managed Identity from a connection string.
Database.0003.title: System.Data.SqlClient dependency detected

HTTP.0001.description:
Your application is trying to reach external resources via HTTP protocol.

For Managed Instance on App Service, secure connectivity to internal/on-prem HTTP services is managed via plan-level VNet integration and private endpoints. Keep inbound traffic restricted to 80/443; capture any required HTTP config in install scripts. Use Bastion JIT RDP only for diagnostics—persist all changes via install scripts. Outbound traffic does not impose similar restrictions.

While if targeting other Azure compute services, ensure the resources it is trying to reach are accessible from the Azure environment. If those HTTP requests are sent to internal services or services hosted on-prem, you probably will need to migrate those services to Azure as well.

Also, keep in mind that Azure App Service supports port 80 for HTTP and port 443 for HTTPS traffic. If you are using any other ports, you need to update them before moving to Azure.
HTTP.0001.title: Access to external resources via HTTP is detected

Local.0006.description:
Your application has hardcoded URLs that might correspond to services that are not accessible on Azure.

Review and ensure that the application is attempting to access URLs that would be available from Azure. You might need to migrate those services to Azure too, this way you will get the benefit of lower latency in the cloud.

If your application needs to depend on services that run on-premises, you can connect your Azure and local networks with ExpressRoute, Hybrid Connections, or VPN solutions.
Local.0006.title: Hardcoded URLs detected

Database.0002.description:
SQL database connection detected.

Ensure the database is available/reachable on Azure.

If it is not, there are multiple ways you can move your data to Azure:
    1. Migrate to Azure SQL Database.
    2. Consider Azure SQL Managed Instance if you need features that are not available in Azure SQL Database and you don’t want to use a VM.
    3. Migrate your database servers directly to SQL Server on Azure VMs.

To assess database migration effort, you can use Azure Migrate tool, see the link below.
Database.0002.title: SQL database connection detected

links.title: Migrate SQL Server database to Azure

Security.0002.description:
Connection strings without configuration builders detected.  

 Keeping connection strings directly in the app is not the best practice and might introduce security risks, increased maintenance, scalability challenges or compliance standards (such as PCI DSS, GDPR, etc.). 

 Instead, it is recommended to use configuration builders to get secrets from secure sources in Azure environment.
Security.0002.title: Connection strings without configuration builders detected

azure-java-version-01000.description:
The application is using a Java version that has reached the end of support. It is strongly recommended to plan and execute a migration strategy to upgrade your application to a supported Java version.
Supported Java versions receive long-term support (LTS) from the Java community, including bug fixes and updates. Migrating to a supported version provides you with a stable and well-maintained platform for your application.
azure-java-version-01000.title: Java Version Has Reached the End of Support

cra-hardcoded-credential-default-pwd-040.description:
**EU Cyber Resilience Act (Annex I, Part I, Section 2(b))** requires a **secure by default configuration**. The use of default, well-known, or trivial passwords violates the secure-by-default principle.

ETSI EN 303 645 (referenced standard for CRA compliance) explicitly prohibits universal default passwords in Provision 5.1-1.

**Recommended actions:**
* Remove all default/well-known passwords from code and configuration.
* Require unique, strong passwords during initial setup.
* Use generated secrets that are unique per deployment.
* Implement password complexity requirements.
cra-hardcoded-credential-default-pwd-040.title: CRA: Default or well-known password detected

links.title: ETSI EN 303 645 - No Universal Default Passwords

hardcoded-urls-00001.description: This patterns verifies in source code and configuration files the presence of hardcoded URLs using the HTTP protocol (HTTP/HTTPS). This URL would need to be replaced by the new resource's URL during the Cloud migration, if any change occured.
hardcoded-urls-00001.title: Avoid using hardcoded URLs (HTTP protocol) in source code

localhost-00004.description: The app is trying to use localhost, please try to migrate the resource to cloud
localhost-00004.title: Localhost Usage

localhost-http-00001.description: The app is trying to access local resource by HTTP, please try to migrate the resource to cloud
localhost-http-00001.title: Local HTTP Calls

unsecure-network-protocol-00000.description:
Using secured protocols such as HTTPS and SFTP (over HTTP and FTP) should now be the norm as applications are more and more exposed and interconnected. 
This CloudReady patterns looks for unescured URI in the source code or deprecated URI libraries. 
Ideally, URLs should be replaced in your source code by secured protocols HTTPS and SFTP (and ensure the infrastructure implements these protocols for the resources your application calls, uses or references).
unsecure-network-protocol-00000.title: Use of unsecured network protocols or URI libraries

links.title: SSH File Transfer Protocol (SFTP): Get SFTP client & server

Database.0003.description:
System.Data.SqlClient dependency is detected.

While System.Data.SqlClient will work in Azure, the newer Microsoft.Data.SqlClient package is the primary .NET SQL client moving forward and receiving the latest features. For example, Microsoft.Data.SqlClient easily allows use of Azure Managed Identity from a connection string.
Database.0003.title: System.Data.SqlClient dependency detected

Security.0003.description:
Hardcoded sensitive data detected.

If you are targeting Managed Instance on Azure App Service, externalize secrets to Key Vault/App Configuration; use managed identity instead of passwords or embedded connection strings.

While if targeting other Azure compute services:

Avoid placing sensitive data such as passwords, ids, tokens etc., directly in config files and code since it compromises the security of your application.

Use Azure App Configuration or Azure KeyVault to store secrets.
Security.0003.title: Hardcoded sensitive data detected

HTTP.0001.description:
Your application is trying to reach external resources via HTTP protocol.

For Managed Instance on App Service, secure connectivity to internal/on-prem HTTP services is managed via plan-level VNet integration and private endpoints. Keep inbound traffic restricted to 80/443; capture any required HTTP config in install scripts. Use Bastion JIT RDP only for diagnostics—persist all changes via install scripts. Outbound traffic does not impose similar restrictions.

While if targeting other Azure compute services, ensure the resources it is trying to reach are accessible from the Azure environment. If those HTTP requests are sent to internal services or services hosted on-prem, you probably will need to migrate those services to Azure as well.

Also, keep in mind that Azure App Service supports port 80 for HTTP and port 443 for HTTPS traffic. If you are using any other ports, you need to update them before moving to Azure.

Local.0006.title: Hardcoded URLs detected

Perf.0001.description:
Synchronous API usage detected.

Using synchronous APIs can lead to more performance issues in Azure compared to on-premises environments due to different latency profiles. Consider adopting asynchronous code patterns.

In heavily loaded systems, especially where the I/O is substantial, using asynchronous patterns can improve overall throughput and performance. Asynchronous APIs enable a small pool of threads to manage thousands of concurrent requests without waiting on blocking calls. Instead of waiting for a long-running synchronous task to finish, the thread can handle another request. Conversely, numerous synchronous blocking calls can cause thread pool starvation and degrade response times.

Refer to the links below to learn more about asynchronous programming.
Perf.0001.title: Synchronous API usage detected

links.title: ASP.NET Core Best Practices: avoid blocking calls

SQL database connection detected.

Ensure the database is available/reachable on Azure.

If it is not, there are multiple ways you can move your data to Azure:
    1. Migrate to Azure SQL Database.
    2. Consider Azure SQL Managed Instance if you need features that are not available in Azure SQL Database and you don’t want to use a VM.
    3. Migrate your database servers directly to SQL Server on Azure VMs.

To assess database migration effort, you can use Azure Migrate tool, see the link below.
Database.0002.title: SQL database connection detected

Database.0003.description:

Local.0007.description:
Local OS environment access detected.

If you are targeting Managed Instance on Azure App Service, Read config via App Settings or App Configuration; avoid machine‑specific environment APIs. Persist environment setup via install scripts when needed.

When running in Azure App Service environment, your application can read environment variables that you define in App Service settings. Using environment API other than reading environment variables might indicate that your application depends on some local machine's features and might be broken on App Service sandbox or not scale well. Review your scenarios and either refactor them to be more cloud friendly or choose the option in App Service to host your application in the sandbox for Windows Container Web Apps.

If you choose to use other Azure hosting options such as Azure Container Apps (ACA) or Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS), environment APIs might work, however scaling still might be an issue.
Local.0007.title: Local OS environment access detected

links.title: App Service sandbox for Windows Container Web Apps

System.Data.SqlClient dependency is detected.

Security.0003.title: Hardcoded sensitive data detected

Scale.0003.description:
MachineKey dependency is detected.

MachineKey is used in ASP.NET apps to protect (encode) or unprotect (decode) data. It will work in Azure environments however it will impact scalability. Data protected by one instance of an application can only be unprotected by other replicas with the same machine key and it is different on other machines.

A suggested replacement of MachineKey is Microsoft.AspNetCore.DataProtection APIs which work for .NET Framework or .NET.
Scale.0003.title: MachineKey dependency is detected

links.title: Replace the ASP.NET machineKey in ASP.NET Core

Security.0001.description:
MD5/SHA1 usage detected. 

 Due to collision problems with MD5/SHA1, Microsoft recommends SHA256 or SHA512. 

 Consider using the SHA256 class or the SHA512 class instead of the MD5 class. Use MD5 only for compatibility with legacy applications and data.
Security.0001.title: MD5/SHA1 usage detected

azure-database-microsoft-sql-03000.description:
To migrate a Java application that uses a Microsoft SQL database to Azure, you can follow these recommendations:

 * Use a managed **Azure SQL**: For that create a managed Azure SQL database in Azure and choose the appropriate pricing tier based on your application's requirements for performance, storage, and availability.

 * **Migrate** the existing Microsoft SQL database: For that you can use the Azure Database Migration Service (DMS) to perform an online migration with minimal downtime.

 * Update the application's **database connection** details: Modify the Java application's configuration to point to the newly provisioned Azure SQL. Update the connection string, hostname, port, username, and password information accordingly.

 * Enable **monitoring and diagnostics**: Utilize Azure Monitor to gain insights into the performance and health of your Java application and the underlying Azure SQL database. Set up metrics, alerts, and log analytics to proactively identify and resolve issues.

 * Implement **security** measures: Apply security best practices to protect your Java application and the Azure SQL database. This includes implementing authentication and authorization mechanisms with passwordless connections and leveraging Microsoft Defender for Cloud for threat detection and vulnerability assessments.

 * **Backup** your data: Azure SQL provides automated backups by default. You can configure the retention period for backups based on your requirements. You can also enable geo-redundant backups, if needed, to enhance data durability and availability.
azure-database-microsoft-sql-03000.title: Microsoft SQL database found

links.title: Change automated backup settings for Azure SQL Database

Supported Java versions receive long-term support (LTS) from the Java community, including bug fixes and updates. Migrating to a supported version provides you with a stable and well-maintained platform for your application.
azure-java-version-01000.title: Java Version Has Reached the End of Support

unsecure-network-protocol-00000.description:
Using secured protocols such as HTTPS and SFTP (over HTTP and FTP) should now be the norm as applications are more and more exposed and interconnected. 
This CloudReady patterns looks for unescured URI in the source code or deprecated URI libraries. 
Ideally, URLs should be replaced in your source code by secured protocols HTTPS and SFTP (and ensure the infrastructure implements these protocols for the resources your application calls, uses or references).
unsecure-network-protocol-00000.title: Use of unsecured network protocols or URI libraries

links.title: SSH File Transfer Protocol (SFTP): Get SFTP client & server

azure-java-version-01000.description:
The application is using a Java version that has reached the end of support. It is strongly recommended to plan and execute a migration strategy to upgrade your application to a supported Java version.
Supported Java versions receive long-term support (LTS) from the Java community, including bug fixes and updates. Migrating to a supported version provides you with a stable and well-maintained platform for your application.
azure-java-version-01000.title: Java Version Has Reached the End of Support

azure-java-version-01000.title: Java Version Has Reached the End of Support

azure-java-version-02000.description:
The application is not using the latest LTS Java version. It is recommended to consider upgrading to the latest LTS version to take advantage of the newest language features, performance improvements, and extended support timelines.
Upgrading to the latest LTS version ensures your application benefits from the most recent security enhancements and a longer support lifecycle.
azure-java-version-02000.title: Java Version is not the latest LTS

hardcoded-urls-00001.description: This patterns verifies in source code and configuration files the presence of hardcoded URLs using the HTTP protocol (HTTP/HTTPS). This URL would need to be replaced by the new resource's URL during the Cloud migration, if any change occured.
hardcoded-urls-00001.title: Avoid using hardcoded URLs (HTTP protocol) in source code

Database.0003.description:
System.Data.SqlClient dependency is detected.

While System.Data.SqlClient will work in Azure, the newer Microsoft.Data.SqlClient package is the primary .NET SQL client moving forward and receiving the latest features. For example, Microsoft.Data.SqlClient easily allows use of Azure Managed Identity from a connection string.
Database.0003.title: System.Data.SqlClient dependency detected

HTTP.0001.description:
Your application is trying to reach external resources via HTTP protocol.

For Managed Instance on App Service, secure connectivity to internal/on-prem HTTP services is managed via plan-level VNet integration and private endpoints. Keep inbound traffic restricted to 80/443; capture any required HTTP config in install scripts. Use Bastion JIT RDP only for diagnostics—persist all changes via install scripts. Outbound traffic does not impose similar restrictions.

While if targeting other Azure compute services, ensure the resources it is trying to reach are accessible from the Azure environment. If those HTTP requests are sent to internal services or services hosted on-prem, you probably will need to migrate those services to Azure as well.

Also, keep in mind that Azure App Service supports port 80 for HTTP and port 443 for HTTPS traffic. If you are using any other ports, you need to update them before moving to Azure.
HTTP.0001.title: Access to external resources via HTTP is detected

Perf.0001.description:
Synchronous API usage detected.

Using synchronous APIs can lead to more performance issues in Azure compared to on-premises environments due to different latency profiles. Consider adopting asynchronous code patterns.

In heavily loaded systems, especially where the I/O is substantial, using asynchronous patterns can improve overall throughput and performance. Asynchronous APIs enable a small pool of threads to manage thousands of concurrent requests without waiting on blocking calls. Instead of waiting for a long-running synchronous task to finish, the thread can handle another request. Conversely, numerous synchronous blocking calls can cause thread pool starvation and degrade response times.

Refer to the links below to learn more about asynchronous programming.
Perf.0001.title: Synchronous API usage detected

links.title: ASP.NET Core Best Practices: avoid blocking calls

Local.0006.description:
Your application has hardcoded URLs that might correspond to services that are not accessible on Azure.

Review and ensure that the application is attempting to access URLs that would be available from Azure. You might need to migrate those services to Azure too, this way you will get the benefit of lower latency in the cloud.

If your application needs to depend on services that run on-premises, you can connect your Azure and local networks with ExpressRoute, Hybrid Connections, or VPN solutions.
Local.0006.title: Hardcoded URLs detected

Database.0002.description:
SQL database connection detected.

Ensure the database is available/reachable on Azure.

If it is not, there are multiple ways you can move your data to Azure:
    1. Migrate to Azure SQL Database.
    2. Consider Azure SQL Managed Instance if you need features that are not available in Azure SQL Database and you don’t want to use a VM.
    3. Migrate your database servers directly to SQL Server on Azure VMs.

To assess database migration effort, you can use Azure Migrate tool, see the link below.
Database.0002.title: SQL database connection detected

links.title: Migrate SQL Server database to Azure

Security.0002.description:
Connection strings without configuration builders detected.  

 Keeping connection strings directly in the app is not the best practice and might introduce security risks, increased maintenance, scalability challenges or compliance standards (such as PCI DSS, GDPR, etc.). 

 Instead, it is recommended to use configuration builders to get secrets from secure sources in Azure environment.
Security.0002.title: Connection strings without configuration builders detected

Security.0002.description:
Connection strings without configuration builders detected.  

 Keeping connection strings directly in the app is not the best practice and might introduce security risks, increased maintenance, scalability challenges or compliance standards (such as PCI DSS, GDPR, etc.). 

 Instead, it is recommended to use configuration builders to get secrets from secure sources in Azure environment.
Security.0002.title: Connection strings without configuration builders detected

Security.0003.description:
Hardcoded sensitive data detected.

If you are targeting Managed Instance on Azure App Service, externalize secrets to Key Vault/App Configuration; use managed identity instead of passwords or embedded connection strings.

While if targeting other Azure compute services:

Avoid placing sensitive data such as passwords, ids, tokens etc., directly in config files and code since it compromises the security of your application.

Use Azure App Configuration or Azure KeyVault to store secrets.
Security.0003.title: Hardcoded sensitive data detected

SMTP.0001.description:
If you are targeting Managed Instance on Azure App Service, use an authenticated SMTP relay (e.g., SendGrid/Exchange Online). Store credentials in Key Vault; validate outbound restrictions for your subscription.

While if targeting other Azure compute services, SMTP connections in your code might not work on Azure. Outbound email messages that are sent directly to external domains are made available only to certain subscription types. We recommend using authenticated SMTP relay services instead.
SMTP.0001.title: SMTP connections detected

links.title: Set up emails with Microsoft 365 or Office 365

Local.0003.description:
Your application has local or network IO operations.

If you are targeting Managed Instance on Azure App Service, move persistent I/O to storage mounts (Azure Files/UNC). Keep local writes to temp only; connect to external dependencies via private endpoints/VNet to reduce latency.

IO operations outside of the application folder might not be available on Azure App Service. Review and ensure IO APIs are attempting to access paths that will be available when your app is running on App Service sandbox. If you choose the containerized hosting option, your scenario still might be broken or have scalability issues.

Possible solutions:
    1. Files that the application needs can be deployed with the application or put in Azure Storage. You can use Azure Storage File Shares as a drop-in replacement for on-premises file share.
    2. Other services that the application depends on might need to be migrated to Azure, this way you will get the benefit of lower latency in the cloud.
    3. If your application needs to depend on services that run on-premise, you can connect your Azure and local networks with ExpressRoute, Hybrid Connections, or VPN solutions.
Local.0003.title: Local or network IO operations detected

Database.0003.title: System.Data.SqlClient dependency detected

cra-hardcoded-credential-default-pwd-040.description:
**EU Cyber Resilience Act (Annex I, Part I, Section 2(b))** requires a **secure by default configuration**. The use of default, well-known, or trivial passwords violates the secure-by-default principle.

ETSI EN 303 645 (referenced standard for CRA compliance) explicitly prohibits universal default passwords in Provision 5.1-1.

**Recommended actions:**
* Remove all default/well-known passwords from code and configuration.
* Require unique, strong passwords during initial setup.
* Use generated secrets that are unique per deployment.
* Implement password complexity requirements.
cra-hardcoded-credential-default-pwd-040.title: CRA: Default or well-known password detected

links.title: ETSI EN 303 645 - No Universal Default Passwords
